---
title: "100_Preliminaries"
format: html

---
	
# This is some markdown

Of course. Let's try a simple R code block:

```{r}
print("Hello!")
```

And now some python, using our installed Conda environment, which RStudio graciously agreed to read from environment.yml? We can hope...

```{python}
import numpy as np
print("Hello!")
print("Numpy version:", np.__version__)
```

Let's see if Python objects persist from block to block.

```{python}
print("Numpy version:", np.__version__)
```
OK, that seemed to work OK.  Let's try some Gemini-provided code to see if we can use some Python created data in R.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create a sample dataframe
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'x': np.arange(1, 11),
    'y': np.random.normal(10, 2, 10) 
})
print(df.head())
```

```{R}
library(ggplot2)
library(reticulate)

# Access the Python object 'df'
my_data <- py$df

# Plot it using ggplot2
ggplot(my_data, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 3) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_minimal()
```

Yes, that worked!  Notice that the library(whatever) lines are roughly the equivalent of import whatever in a python script or Jupyter notebook.  In the case of reticulate, that needed to be added to get the "py" object added to the namespace.
